unitTitle: KS2 Y3 Connecting computers (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: How does a digital device work?
lessons.objectives:
I can explain that a digital device takes an input
I can explain that a digital device gives an output
I can follow the input → process → output steps
I can explain what makes a password strong
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: digital device · input · process · output · password. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; a board computer ready to type on. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — what is a digital device? Pupils CHOOSE that a digital device does a job (not just on/off), then tick which things are digital devices on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: yes/no is each thing a digital device.  • Core: name a device you use.  • Challenge: multi-select the digital devices (laptop, console, smartphone — not pencil/spoon).
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — input, process, output. Type a letter on the board: input = my finger on the key, process = the computer works it out, output = the letter on screen. Optional hand-jive (in=point down, process=roll hands, out=point out). Likely error: swapping input and output → fix-words “input goes IN first, output comes OUT last.”
3. (20–35) You-do — order the steps. Pupils drag input → process → output into order on the activity worksheet (the `order` task), then answer the Support tick / Core explain questions.
4. (35–48) Strong passwords. A password keeps a device safe; strong = long and hard to guess, letters AND numbers. Challenge pupils multi-select what makes a password strong (not your name, not “password”). No real passwords shared aloud.
5. (48–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil names one input and one output.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: What parts make up a digital device?
lessons.objectives:
I can sort input and output devices
I can name the parts of a computer
I can describe a simple process (input → process → output)
I can design my own digital device
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: input · output · process · device. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the labelling image ready. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — label the computer. Pupils drag Screen / Base unit / Keyboard / Mouse onto the computer image (the `label` task), then choose which part you type with / look at / click with.
   • Support: which part for each job.  • Core: keyboard = input, screen = output.  • Challenge: name an input and an output on a tablet.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — input or output? Input goes IN (keyboard, mouse, microphone); output comes OUT (screen, speaker, printer). Name a device, pupils say input or output. Likely error: calling a speaker an input → fix-words “the sound comes OUT, so it is an output.”
3. (25–35) You-do — sort the devices. Pupils drag devices into INPUT / OUTPUT on the activity worksheet (the `sort` task). Some fit BOTH (touchscreen) or NEITHER (cable) — saved for Challenge multi-select.
4. (35–50) Design your own digital device. Pupils invent a device with at least one input and one output, draw or build it on screen, and record it in show-your-work. Low floor: one input + one output. High ceiling: many.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil names their device’s input and output.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: How do digital devices help us?
lessons.objectives:
I can name jobs I can do on a digital device
I can make art on a computer using brush, fill and undo
I can say what is the same using a computer and using paper
I can say what is different using a computer and using paper
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: program · digital · non-digital. TA: a paint program (e.g. Paint, JS Paint) open for everyone; paper and pencils ready. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what can a device do? A program is the set of instructions that does a job. Pupils tick the real jobs (write, play, paint, message — not “eat an apple”) on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: yes/no can you do this on a tablet.  • Core: multi-select real jobs.  • Challenge: name a job that is easier on a computer.
2. (10–25) I-do — the paint tools. Model brush (draw), fill (colour a shape), undo (take back a mistake). Likely error: pressing many buttons fast → fix-words “one tool at a time.”
3. (25–45) You-do — make a picture digitally. Pupils make a picture with brush/fill/undo and screenshot it for show-your-work. Low floor: a filled shape. High ceiling: a detailed scene. No flashing effects.
4. (45–52) Same and different. Compare with paper: SAME = choose colours, make a picture; DIFFERENT = computer has undo, paper can smudge. Core pupils write one of each; Challenge multi-select true sentences.
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil says which they preferred and why.
Note: this can run as an art lesson + a computing lesson if you want both pictures made. Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: How am I connected?
lessons.objectives:
I can say what a connection is
I can say what a network is
I can show how a message goes from one computer to another
I can say why a network switch helps
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: connection · network · switch. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; string/rope ready for the role-play. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what is a connection? A connection is a LINK between two things so they can share. Pupils choose the meaning and tick which pairs are connected.
   • Support: yes/no is each pair connected.  • Core: name two people you are connected to.  • Challenge: many things connected = a network.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — connection → network → switch. Move from people you’d tell good news to computers joined together. A network = many things connected. Joining everyone to everyone needs lots of cables; instead join all to ONE switch in the middle. The switch passes each message to the right computer and does NOT read it.
3. (25–40) You-do — order the message journey. Pupils drag computer 1 → switch → computer 2 into order (the `order` task). Likely error: thinking the message goes through other computers → fix-words “it goes to the switch, then straight to the right computer.”
4. (40–50) Be the network. Calm role-play with string for connections: pass a message to a friend THROUGH the switch. Screenshot/photo for show-your-work. Core explain why a switch helps; Challenge multi-select true sentences.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil says what the switch does.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: How are computers connected?
lessons.objectives:
I can name the devices in a network (switch, server, wireless access point)
I can say what a switch does
I can say what a server does
I can say what a wireless access point does
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: switch · server · wireless access point. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; string/rope and role-play cards ready. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — remember the switch. Pupils choose what a switch does (passes messages to the right computer) and recap network/switch.
   • Support: which device passes messages.  • Core: why connect computers into a network.  • Challenge: a network can have more than just a switch (true).
2. (8–25) I-do → we-do — three devices. Switch = passes messages; server = a strong computer that stores files for everyone; wireless access point = lets tablets/laptops join with NO cable (Wi-Fi). Likely error: mixing up server and switch → fix-words “Server Stores, Switch Sends.”
3. (25–38) You-do — match device to job. Pupils match switch / server / wireless access point to their job on the activity worksheet (matching grid). Support recognition choices; Core explain why the server is useful.
4. (38–50) Be the network. Role-play switch, server and computers; send a file to the server through the switch. Challenge pupils order the file journey (the `order` task). Screenshot/photo for show-your-work.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil says what the server does.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: What does our school network look like?
lessons.objectives:
I can name the parts of our school network
I can say how the parts are joined (cables and sockets)
I can find networked devices around school
I can say why a network is useful
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: network cable · network socket. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; tour route planned; IT technician invited if possible; a camera/tablet for photos. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — remember the three devices. Pupils match the picture of switch / server / wireless access point to its name (Support matching grid), and recall what each does.
   • Support: match picture to name.  • Core: name a networked device in school.  • Challenge: a network lets many people share files (true).
2. (8–20) I-do — cables and sockets. A network cable carries information; it plugs into a network socket in the wall. A printer is a networked device too — many computers, one printer.
3. (20–30) You-do — wired or wireless? Pupils sort devices into joins-with-a-cable / joins-with-Wi-Fi (the `sort` task). Likely error: thinking a tablet uses a cable → fix-words “a tablet has no cable, it uses Wi-Fi.”
4. (30–48) Network tour. Calm, structured walk with a clear route and a spotting list (computers, printer, wireless access points, cables, sockets, switch, server). Pupils photograph one part for show-your-work. Movement is part of the routine.
5. (48–55) Plenary — why is a network useful? Share files, use the same printer, save work on the server. Challenge multi-select the benefits. Self-tick the four “I can…”.
Note: a computer does not need a local network to reach the internet — keep that simple; the internet is the focus in Year 4. Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
